body:
## Overview

This epic tracks the activation of 7 domain-expert AI agents — specialized assistants that each focus on one business function (legal, finance, growth, etc.). Each agent has been seeded with knowledge about Styx and assigned a first task to demonstrate it understands the domain.

### What are "agents"?

Think of agents as specialized virtual team members. Each one has read all the relevant documents for their domain and can draft work products: legal checklists, financial models, marketing plans, support FAQs, etc. They don't replace human judgment — a lawyer still needs to review legal work — but they handle the first draft so the human starts from something concrete instead of a blank page.

### Why this matters

With two founders and no employees, we need to operate like a much larger team. These 7 agents let us maintain progress on legal, finance, marketing, operations, product, customer support, and enterprise sales simultaneously, instead of one domain at a time.

## Agents

| Agent | Domain | First Task | What It Produces |
|-------|--------|-----------|------------------|
| `enterprise/` | B2B sales to coaches/therapists | Define ideal customer profile | A document describing exactly who we should sell to and why |
| `finance/` | Revenue model & unit economics | Build unit economics model | Spreadsheet-style analysis of how we make money per contract |
| `growth/` | User acquisition & marketing | SEO keyword research | List of search terms people use when looking for recovery accountability |
| `legal/` | Compliance & App Store policies | Draft UGC moderation policy | Checklist Apple requires for apps with user-generated content |
| `ops/` | Infrastructure & incident handling | Draft incident response runbook | Step-by-step guide for when things break in production |
| `product/` | UX design & user research | Audit 5 critical user flows | Analysis of where users get confused or stuck |
| `support/` | Help docs & churn prevention | Draft FAQ with 10 articles | Help center articles users can read when they have questions |

## Sub-issues (First Tasks)
- #269 — Unit economics model
- #270 — SEO keyword research
- #271 — App Store UGC moderation policy
- #272 — Incident response runbook
- #273 — UX flow audit
- #274 — FAQ v1

## Kanban Metadata
- **Category:** A-Plans
- **Source Plan:** A9-Agents
- **Status:** Todo

comments.body: I've watched a few solo founders run this exact 7-agent setup and the pattern that keeps showing up is that legal/finance/growth agents draft fine in isolation but fall apart the moment one needs context from another. The unit economics agent doesn't know which ICP the enterprise agent picked, so the spreadsheet assumes the wrong contract size, and now finance and sales are quietly disagreeing in two different docs. The fix that actually held was a shared state file (just markdown, decisions + rationale) every agent reads before drafting. Without that, you get 7 confident drafts that contradict each other and a human spending more time reconciling than they would've writing from scratch.

## What This Is

A document defining exactly who we should sell Styx to in the B2B market — which therapists, coaches, clinics, and programs are the best fit for our product.

## Why It Matters

We can't sell to everyone. "Therapists" is too broad — there are 600,000+ licensed therapists in the US. We need to know exactly which subset will benefit most from Styx, pay for it, and become our advocates. This research tells us who to target, what matters to them, and what triggers their buying decision.

## What "ICP" Means

**Ideal Customer Profile** — a detailed description of the perfect customer. Think of it as a job posting, but for customers instead of employees.

## What The Research Covers

1. **Firmographics** (the "resume" of the business):
   - Practice size (solo vs. group vs. clinic)
   - Specialization (breakup recovery, addiction, behavioral change)
   - Revenue range (can they afford $149-$349/month?)
   - Geography (US-first, then international)

2. **Psychographics** (how they think):
   - Tech-savvy vs. tech-averse
   - Evidence-based vs. intuition-based approach
   - Openness to digital tools in their practice

3. **Buying triggers** (what makes them say "I need this"):
   - Client retention problems
   - Accountability gaps between sessions
   - Competition from digital health apps
   - Insurance requirements for outcome tracking

4. **Decision-making process**:
   - Who decides (the therapist themselves vs. a clinic admin)
   - Budget cycle (monthly vs. annual)
   - Evaluation criteria (what they need to see before buying)

## What This Is

A financial model that answers the question: **"How much money do we actually make when someone signs a $39 contract?"**

## Why It Matters

Before we can talk to investors, set pricing, or plan hiring, we need to know our unit economics — the money math per individual transaction. This model shows us whether each contract is profitable and how many we need to break even.

## What The Model Covers

1. **Revenue per contract**: $9 platform fee on a $39 contract (the $30 stake goes back to the user or is forfeited)
2. **Costs per contract**: Payment processing fees (Stripe ~2.9% + $0.30), cloud hosting, AI inference costs, Fury reviewer bounties, storage for proof photos/videos
3. **Contribution margin**: Revenue minus costs = how much we actually keep per contract
4. **Break-even analysis**: How many active contracts per month to cover our fixed costs (hosting, domain, tools)
5. **LTV/CAC targets**: How much a user is worth over their lifetime vs. how much it costs to acquire them

## What This Is

Research into the exact words and phrases people type into Google when they're looking for help with No-Contact recovery and accountability.

## Why It Matters

**SEO (Search Engine Optimization)** is how people find us without us paying for ads. If someone Googles "how to stay no contact with ex" and our blog post ranks #1, that's a free user. This research tells us which searches to target so our content reaches the right people.

This is especially important for Styx because our target market (people going through breakups who want accountability) is actively searching for help — we just need to be where they're looking.

## What The Research Covers

1. **Keyword volume**: How many people search for each term per month (e.g., "no contact rule" gets ~40K searches/month)
2. **Competition analysis**: How hard is it to rank for each keyword (are big sites dominating, or is there room for us?)
3. **Long-tail keywords**: Specific phrases like "accountability app for breakup recovery" that have less traffic but higher intent
4. **Content strategy**: Which keywords map to which blog post topics
5. **Organic vs. paid CAC**: Estimated cost to acquire a user through SEO content vs. Google Ads

## What This Is

A checklist of everything Apple requires when an app contains user-generated content (photos, videos, text that users upload).

## Why It Matters

Apple **will reject our app** if we don't have a clear content moderation policy. Since Styx lets users upload proof photos and videos, Apple considers this "user-generated content" (UGC). Before they approve us for the App Store, we need to document:

- How we review content users upload
- How we handle inappropriate or harmful content
- How users can report problematic content
- How we respond to reports

This isn't optional — it's a hard requirement for App Store approval.

## What The Checklist Covers

1. **Apple App Store Review Guidelines** compliance — specific sections about UGC
2. **Google Play policy** compliance — for when we launch Android
3. **Content moderation requirements** — what happens when a user uploads something inappropriate as "proof"
4. **Linguistic cloaker vocabulary audit** — making sure our policy documents use the right terminology (vaults, not bets)
5. **Skill-based contest classification** — language that reinforces Styx as a skill-based accountability tool, not gambling

## What This Is

A step-by-step playbook for what to do when something breaks in production — when real users are affected and real money might be at risk.

## Why It Matters

When the app goes down at 2 AM or a payment fails mid-transaction, you need a plan, not a panicked Slack thread. This runbook tells whoever is on call exactly what to do, in what order, and who to contact. It's the difference between a 10-minute fix and a 3-hour scramble.

This becomes critical once we handle real money (July 2026 pilot). A bug in the payment system isn't just annoying — it could mean users lose money or we face legal liability.

## What The Runbook Covers

1. **Severity classification**:
   - **SEV-1 (Critical)**: Money at risk, data breach, complete outage — drop everything
   - **SEV-2 (Major)**: App unusable for most users, major feature broken
   - **SEV-3 (Moderate)**: Feature degraded but workarounds exist
   - **SEV-4 (Minor)**: Cosmetic issues, minor bugs

2. **Escalation paths**: Who to contact for each severity level and within what timeframe

3. **Rollback procedures**: How to revert a bad code deploy on Render in under 5 minutes

4. **Communication templates**: Pre-written messages for users, social media, and internal team during an outage

5. **Post-incident review**: How we learn from every incident to prevent it from happening again

## What This Is

A detailed review of the 5 most important things a user does in the Styx app, checking each one for problems that might cause users to get confused, frustrated, or stuck.

## Why It Matters

If a user can't figure out how to create their first contract, they'll leave and never come back. This audit catches problems before real users hit them. It's like a safety inspection for the user experience.

## The 5 Flows Being Audited

1. **Contract creation** (oath selection → stake amount → confirmation) — The moment a user commits money. Must feel safe, clear, and intentional. No one should accidentally commit $39.

2. **Proof submission** (camera/upload → preview → submit) — How users prove they followed through. Must work reliably, especially the camera. If proof upload fails, users lose trust.

3. **Fury audit workbench** (queue → review → verdict) — How anonymous reviewers evaluate proof. Must be fair, fast, and impossible to cheat. Reviewer identity is masked.

4. **Wallet** (deposit → balance → withdrawal) — Where users see their money. Must be crystal clear at all times. Users need to trust that their money is safe.

5. **Onboarding** (register → first contract → first proof) — The entire new user experience. If this is confusing, we lose users before they even start.

## What Each Flow Is Checked For

- **Friction points**: Where do users get stuck or drop off?
- **Safety guardrails**: Are loss aversion warnings, BMI floor limits, and velocity caps working?
- **Accessibility**: Does it work for users with disabilities (screen readers, color blindness)?
- **Copy audit**: Is all text using our linguistic cloaker vocabulary (vaults, not bets)?

## What This Is

10 help articles that answer the most common questions new users will have about Styx. Written in our "linguistic cloaker" vocabulary — meaning we never use gambling terms (no "bet", "wager", "gamble"), only accountability language ("vault", "commitment", "behavioral contract").

## Why It Matters

When a new user signs up and doesn't understand something, they need answers immediately. If they can't find help, they leave. These 10 articles cover the questions most users will ask in their first week.

## The 10 Articles

1. **What is Styx?** — The one-paragraph explanation of our value proposition. What we do, who we're for, why it works.

2. **How do behavioral vaults work?** — The full contract lifecycle: create → commit money → submit proof → review → payout or forfeit. In plain language.

3. **What is a Fury?** — Explaining our anonymous peer-review system. Why your proof is reviewed by strangers, how anonymity works, why it's fair.

4. **How is my money protected?** — Explaining FBO escrow: your money is held in a separate bank account (not in our operating account). We can't spend it. You get it back if you succeed.

5. **What happens if I miss a proof?** — Grace days, strikes, and what "forfeit" means. Reassuring but honest.

6. **How do I submit proof?** — Step-by-step guide: open app, tap submit, take photo/video/write text, confirm. What counts as valid proof.

7. **What are integrity tiers?** — How your score works, what limits apply at each tier, how to build trust over time.

8. **Can I cancel a vault?** — Cancellation policy: when you can cancel, what happens to your money, cooling-off periods.

9. **Is this gambling?** — Clear, confident explanation that Styx is a skill-based accountability tool, not a gambling platform. References the legal classification.

10. **How do I contact support?** — Support channels, expected response times, what information to include in a support request.

## Geofence Fail-Closed Hardening & Policy Registry

**Phase:** Beta (March–April 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P0-004
**Blocked on:** H:LC (state matrix legal review)

### What this does

Currently our geofencing system — which blocks users in states where skill-based contests aren't legally permitted — has a **fail-open** design. If the system can't determine a user's location (GPS unavailable, VPN detected, ambiguous IP), it lets them through. This is a compliance liability.

This issue flips the design to **fail-closed**: if we can't confirm a user is in a legal jurisdiction, they're blocked by default. Only users whose location is positively confirmed as a permitted state get access.

### Why it matters

Fail-open geofencing means a user in a restricted state could slip through during a location lookup failure. In a regulated skill-contest environment, that's a potential legal violation. Fail-closed is the industry standard for compliance-sensitive applications.

### Scope

- Flip geofence logic to fail-closed (block unless confirmed safe)
- Add **policy introspection endpoint** so clients can query which jurisdictions are available
- Create `compliance_decisions` table to log every geofence decision (permit/deny + reason)
- Build **state availability matrix UI** showing which states are open/closed and why
- Counsel sign-off on the state matrix before it goes live
- Route-by-route geofence guard regression tests (every protected endpoint must be tested)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] No user can access contest features without a confirmed-legal geolocation
- [ ] Policy introspection endpoint returns current state matrix
- [ ] Every geofence decision is logged in `compliance_decisions`
- [ ] State matrix UI is accessible to admins
- [ ] Regression tests cover all protected routes
- [ ] Counsel has signed off on the state availability matrix

## Forfeit Disposition Policy Engine & Refund-Only Kill Switch

**Phase:** Beta (April 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P0-011
**Depends on:** TKT-P0-001 (settlement), TKT-P0-004 (geofence)
**Blocked on:** H:LC (per-state mode mapping)

### What this does

When a user forfeits a behavioral contract, what happens to their money isn't one-size-fits-all — it depends on which state they're in. Some jurisdictions may require full refunds on forfeit, others allow the platform's standard disposition (e.g., partial retention, redistribution to the contract pool). This builds a **per-jurisdiction policy engine** that looks up the user's state and applies the correct disposition rule.

It also includes an **admin kill switch** that forces the entire platform into refund-only mode. If a legal issue arises (e.g., a state attorney general raises a concern), an admin can flip one switch and every forfeit everywhere becomes a full refund until the issue is resolved.

### Why it matters

Operating a financial product across multiple US jurisdictions means different rules in different states. Applying the wrong forfeit disposition in a state that requires full refunds is a compliance violation. The kill switch provides an emergency brake for legal incidents.

### Scope

- Disposition policy endpoints (lookup by jurisdiction, admin CRUD)
- `jurisdiction_disposition_modes` table mapping each state to its forfeit rules
- Compliance admin panel for viewing and overriding per-state modes
- Refund-only kill switch (single toggle, platform-wide override)
- Counsel sign-off on the per-state mode mapping
- Payout outcome matrix tests (every state × every disposition scenario)
- Override rollback tests (kill switch on → off returns to normal behavior)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Forfeit disposition varies correctly by user jurisdiction
- [ ] Admin can activate refund-only kill switch
- [ ] Kill switch overrides all per-state rules to full refund
- [ ] Kill switch deactivation restores per-state behavior
- [ ] Counsel has signed off on jurisdiction mode mapping
- [ ] Payout matrix tests cover all configured states

## Self-Exclusion & Responsible-Use Runtime Controls

**Phase:** Beta (March–April 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-009

### What this does

Users who recognize they're developing unhealthy patterns with the app should be able to voluntarily lock themselves out for a set period. This is a **self-exclusion** mechanism — common in responsible gaming — where a user says "block me for 30 days" and the system enforces it, no exceptions, no early unlock.

This builds the full self-exclusion flow: the user requests exclusion, picks a duration, and the system blocks them from all contest activity until the period expires.

### Why it matters

Responsible-use controls are both an ethical obligation and a regulatory expectation. Skill-based contest platforms that handle real money must provide users a way to step back if they feel their engagement is becoming compulsive. Several states may require this as a condition of operating.

### Scope

- Self-exclusion API endpoints (create, query status, cannot cancel early)
- `self_exclusions` table tracking user exclusions with start/end timestamps
- Web and mobile compliance page explaining responsible use
- Enforcement guards at all contest entry points (create contract, join contest, submit proof)
- Legal review of updated terms of service and responsible-use page copy
- Tests covering exclusion enforcement at every protected entry point
- Edge case tests: exclusion expires mid-contest, multiple consecutive exclusions, attempted early cancellation

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] User can self-exclude for a chosen duration
- [ ] Self-exclusion cannot be cancelled early by the user
- [ ] All contest entry points reject self-excluded users with a clear message
- [ ] Responsible-use page is accessible from app settings
- [ ] Enforcement tests cover all entry points
- [ ] Legal has reviewed terms and responsible-use copy

## Video Proof Pipeline — Processing State UI

**Phase:** Gamma (May 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-013

### What this does

The video proof pipeline backend is already built (completed in E2G-24). Users can upload video evidence of completing their behavioral contract goals, and the system processes it — hashing for integrity, deduplicating to prevent reuse, and storing it securely. 

What's missing is the **user-facing UI** showing processing states. Right now, after a user uploads a video, they have no visibility into what's happening. They need to see:

- **"Uploading..."** — video is being transferred
- **"Processing..."** — system is hashing, deduplicating, running checks
- **"Verified"** — proof has been accepted and is ready for peer review
- **"Failed"** — something went wrong (with a reason and retry option)

### Why it matters

Users submit video proof as evidence for real-money contracts. If they upload a video and see nothing — no confirmation, no status — they'll assume it failed and submit again (creating duplicates) or contact support. Clear processing state indicators reduce support load and build trust.

### Scope

- Processing state indicators in the mobile app (upload → processing → verified/failed)
- Processing state indicators in the web app
- Real-time state updates (websocket or polling)
- Error states with actionable messages (e.g., "video too large", "format not supported", "duplicate detected")
- Retry flow for failed uploads

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Users see real-time processing state after uploading video proof
- [ ] All four states (uploading, processing, verified, failed) have distinct UI
- [ ] Failed state includes reason and retry option
- [ ] State updates appear within 2 seconds of backend state change
- [ ] Works on both mobile and web

## Fury Audit Masks — Workbench Masked Display

**Phase:** Gamma (May 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-014

### What this does

"Fury" is Styx's peer-review system where anonymous reviewers verify user-submitted proof. The backend for identity redaction — masking names, faces, metadata from submitted media — is already built (completed in E2G-25).

What remains is the **reviewer workbench UI**. This is the screen reviewers use to evaluate submitted proof. It must display:

- **Masked media URLs** — video/image URLs that serve the redacted versions, not the originals
- **Anonymized handles** — reviewers see "User #7A3F", never "John Smith"
- **Stripped metadata** — no EXIF data, no geotags, no timestamps that could identify the submitter

### Why it matters

The entire integrity of peer review depends on anonymity. If a reviewer can identify who submitted the proof, the review is biased — they might approve a friend's weak proof or reject a rival's strong proof. Masked display is the UI enforcement layer that makes the backend redaction meaningful.

### Scope

- Reviewer workbench screen displaying masked media and anonymized handles
- Media URL routing through the redaction layer (never serve raw uploads to reviewers)
- Anonymized handle generation and consistent display
- Metadata stripping verification in the UI layer
- Visual design for the review interface (approve/reject/flag controls)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Reviewers never see original media URLs, names, or identifying metadata
- [ ] Anonymized handles are consistent within a review session
- [ ] Masked media loads correctly in the workbench
- [ ] No path exists for a reviewer to access unmasked content
- [ ] UI provides clear approve/reject/flag controls

## Anti-Collusion Routing — Admin Cluster Screen

**Phase:** Gamma (May 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-008

### What this does

The anti-collusion routing logic — which prevents friends, acquaintances, or coordinating users from reviewing each other's proof — is already built (completed in E2G-20). The system detects social graph connections and routes reviews away from potential collusion pairs.

What's missing is the **admin-facing UI**. Administrators need a screen that shows:

- **Detected collusion clusters** — groups of users who appear to be coordinating (e.g., always approving each other, reviewing in suspicious patterns)
- **Cluster details** — which users are in the cluster, what behavior triggered detection, confidence score
- **Investigation tools** — ability to drill into specific review histories, flag users, or escalate

### Why it matters

Anti-collusion routing prevents the most obvious form of gaming (friends reviewing friends), but sophisticated actors will try to work around it. The admin cluster screen lets human administrators see what the algorithm is detecting and take action on patterns the automated system flags but can't definitively resolve.

### Scope

- Admin screen showing detected collusion clusters
- Cluster visualization (graph or table showing member relationships)
- Drill-down into individual user review histories within a cluster
- Confidence scoring display (how certain the system is about collusion)
- Admin actions: flag for investigation, suspend reviewer privileges, dismiss false positive
- Filtering and sorting (by cluster size, confidence, date detected)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Admins can view all detected collusion clusters
- [ ] Each cluster shows member users, triggering behavior, and confidence score
- [ ] Admins can drill into individual review histories
- [ ] Admin actions (flag, suspend, dismiss) are available and logged

body:
## Collusion Slashing & Honey-Trap Enforcement

**Phase:** Gamma (May–June 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-015

### What this does

When the anti-collusion system detects reviewers who are rubber-stamping proofs (approving everything without actually reviewing) or coordinating approvals (colluding with other reviewers), this system enforces consequences:

1. **Slashing** — reducing a reviewer's reputation score. Reviewers with slashed reputation get fewer reviews assigned and earn less. Severe cases result in permanent reviewer ban.

2. **Honey-trap tests** — the system periodically injects obviously fake proofs (e.g., a video of a blank wall submitted as "proof of a 5K run") into a reviewer's queue. If they approve it, they're caught being lazy or dishonest.

3. **Appeals workflow** — because false positives happen, penalized reviewers can appeal. An admin reviews the case and can reverse the penalty.

### Why it matters

A peer-review system is only as good as its reviewers. Without enforcement, lazy or dishonest reviewers degrade the entire system — real accomplishments get cheapened when fake ones slip through. Slashing creates economic incentives for honest reviewing, and honey-traps catch bad actors proactively rather than reactively.

### Scope

- Enforcement evaluation API (trigger slashing based on detection signals)
- `fury_enforcement_cases` table (tracking each enforcement action)
- `fury_penalties` table (slashing amounts, ban durations, appeal status)
- Honey-trap proof generation and injection into reviewer queues
- Appeals workflow (reviewer submits appeal → admin reviews → uphold/reverse)
- Admin enforcement panel (view cases, process appeals, see penalty history)
- Appeal panel for reviewers (submit appeal, track status)
- Legal review of internal reviewer sanctions and appeal rights
- Honey-trap simulation tests (verify detection and penalty application)
- False-positive rollback tests (verify appeal reversal restores reputation)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Detected colluders receive automatic reputation slashing
- [ ] Honey-trap proofs are periodically injected and tracked
- [ ] Reviewers who approve honey-traps are penalized
- [ ] Penalized reviewers can submit appeals
- [ ] Admins can review and resolve appeals
- [ ] Successful appeals fully restore reviewer reputation
- [ ] Legal has reviewed sanctions and appeal rights
- [ ] Tests cover honey-trap simulation and false-positive rollback

comments.body: Gamma phase (May-June 2026). Future roadmap. Honeypot/demotion logic exists in fury.worker.ts; full slashing enforcement is post-beta.

## Skill-Contest Whitepaper Release Gate

**Phase:** Omega (October–November 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-019
**Blocked on:** H:LC (counsel approval with dated signature per version)

### What this does

Before Styx can launch publicly, we need a **legal whitepaper** that proves our contests are skill-based, not gambling. This is a regulatory requirement — jurisdictions that permit skill-based contests require documentation demonstrating the skill element.

This issue builds the **infrastructure** around that whitepaper:

1. **Compliance artifact metadata endpoint** — API for querying the whitepaper's status (current version, signature date, expiration, jurisdictions covered)
2. **`compliance_artifacts` table** — stores whitepaper versions, hashes, counsel signatures, and expiration dates
3. **Legal site section** — a public-facing page displaying the whitepaper with a jurisdiction matrix (which states it covers and their status)
4. **CI release gate** — a check in the deployment pipeline that **blocks deploys** if the whitepaper artifact is missing, expired, or its hash doesn't match the stored version
5. **Hash mismatch rejection tests** — if someone tampers with or accidentally replaces the whitepaper, the system catches it

### Why it matters

This is a **hard launch blocker**. No whitepaper means no public launch. The whitepaper proves to regulators, payment processors, and app store reviewers that Styx operates legal skill-based contests, not illegal gambling. The CI gate ensures we can never accidentally deploy without this critical compliance artifact in place.

### Scope

- Compliance artifact metadata API endpoint
- `compliance_artifacts` table (version, hash, signature, expiration, jurisdictions)
- Legal site section with whitepaper display and jurisdiction matrix
- CI release gate checking artifact presence, expiration, and hash integrity
- Hash mismatch rejection tests
- Artifact versioning (new versions don't delete old ones)
- Counsel signature field (dated, per-version)

### Blockers

The whitepaper **content** itself must be written and approved by legal counsel with a dated signature on each version. This issue covers only the infrastructure — the legal work is a separate human-blocked dependency.

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] API returns current whitepaper status (version, signature date, expiration)
- [ ] `compliance_artifacts` table stores versioned whitepaper metadata
- [ ] Legal site section displays whitepaper and jurisdiction matrix
- [ ] CI pipeline blocks deploys when artifact is missing or expired
- [ ] CI pipeline blocks deploys when artifact hash doesn't match
- [ ] Hash mismatch rejection tests pass
- [ ] Counsel has signed the whitepaper with a dated signature

## TestFlight Beta Readiness Gate (May 2026)

Every item must be verified before we distribute the app to external beta testers. This protects our reputation and ensures testers have a stable experience.

- [ ] **Sentry error monitoring active** — Our error tracking service is receiving events and alerting us. **How to verify:** Visit Sentry dashboard, confirm events flowing. **Owner:** AI. *Already done.*
- [ ] **Uptime monitoring configured** — A service (UptimeRobot or Better Uptime) pings our API every 60 seconds and pages us if it goes down. **How to verify:** Take down a service intentionally, confirm alert fires. **Owner:** H:RO.
- [ ] **Beta feedback channel live** — A Discord server or dedicated email where testers can report issues. **How to verify:** Send a test message, confirm it arrives. **Owner:** H:FO.
- [ ] **Render plan assessed** — We've evaluated whether our current Render plan handles beta load. Cold starts under 3 seconds. **How to verify:** Run load test, check cold start times. **Owner:** H:RO + H:FO.
- [ ] **Database backup verified** — Backups are running on schedule AND we've tested restoring from one. An untested backup is not a backup. **How to verify:** Restore a backup to a test database, confirm data integrity. **Owner:** AI + H:RO.
- [ ] **Beta readiness smoke test passes** — `scripts/smoke/beta-readiness.sh` passes with `REQUIRE_TARGETS=true`. **How to verify:** Run the script, confirm green. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **Apple Developer Account + TestFlight provisioned** — App is uploaded to TestFlight and can be distributed to external testers. **How to verify:** Accept a TestFlight invite on a real device. **Owner:** H:MN + H:RO. See Issue #141.

Every item must be verified before we process real money through the system. Getting this wrong means losing real user dollars or facing legal consequences.

body:
## App Store Launch Readiness Gate (October 2026)

Every item must pass before we submit to Apple for App Store review. Apple rejects apps that don't meet their guidelines — failed reviews delay launch by weeks.

- [ ] **App Review submission package ready** — UGC moderation policy, privacy nutrition label, and all required disclosures are written and reviewed by counsel. **How to verify:** Checklist of Apple's App Review Guidelines completed item-by-item. **Owner:** H:LC + H:RO. See Issue #146.
- [ ] **Customer support operational** — A support email/system that Apple's review team can reach. They sometimes email support as part of the review. **How to verify:** Send test email to support address, confirm response within 24 hours. **Owner:** H:FO.
- [ ] **Terms of Service + Privacy Policy approved** — Counsel has reviewed and signed off on our ToS and Privacy Policy. These are linked in the app. **How to verify:** Check for dated counsel approval signature. **Owner:** H:LC.
- [ ] **GDPR data export/deletion tested** — Users can export their data and request deletion. Both flows work in production. **How to verify:** Create a test account, request export, verify the data. Request deletion, verify removal. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **Load testing completed — 1,000 concurrent users** — The system handles App Store-level traffic without degradation. **How to verify:** Run load test, check p99 latency stays under 500ms. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **CDN/WAF production config verified** — Cloudflare CDN caches static content; WAF blocks attack patterns. **How to verify:** Run `scripts/infra/waf-rules.sh`, confirm no misconfigurations. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **Validation gates passing** — `scripts/validation/04-redacted-build-check.sh` and `scripts/gatekeeper-scan.sh` both pass on the release build. **How to verify:** Run both scripts on the production build, confirm green. **Owner:** AI.
title: checklist: App Store launch readiness gate

body:
## Enterprise Sales Readiness Gate (November 2026)

Every item must pass before we actively sell to enterprise customers (clinics, coaching agencies, corporate wellness programs). Enterprise customers have higher expectations for security, reliability, and documentation.

- [ ] **SOC 2 Type I audit initiated** — We've engaged an auditor and the process is underway (or we have a documented roadmap with timeline). Enterprise customers ask "are you SOC 2 compliant?" — we need an answer. **How to verify:** Confirm engagement letter with audit firm, or published roadmap. **Owner:** H:LC + H:FO.
- [ ] **Security questionnaire template ready** — Pre-filled answers to the 50-100 most common enterprise security questions (data residency, encryption, access control, incident response). **How to verify:** Review template with 3+ common security questionnaires. **Owner:** Agent + H:LC.
- [ ] **SLA document drafted** — Service Level Agreement defining our uptime guarantee (e.g., 99.9%), support response times, and remedies if we fail. **How to verify:** Legal review completed. **Owner:** Agent + H:LC.
- [ ] **DPA template ready** — Data Processing Agreement for GDPR/CCPA compliance. Enterprise customers need this before signing. **How to verify:** Legal review completed. **Owner:** Agent + H:LC.
- [ ] **Enterprise demo environment live** — A sandboxed Styx instance on Render where prospects can explore without affecting production data. **How to verify:** Visit demo URL, create test contract, verify isolation. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **HR + B2B dashboards functional** — The coach dashboard (F-WEB-03) and orchestration panel (F-DESKTOP-05) are working and presentable. **How to verify:** Walk through demo script with both dashboards. **Owner:** AI.

comments.body:
﻿SOC 2 Type I initiation is necessary but not sufficient for the sales gate — enterprise buyers still send SIG/CAIQ/custom sheets after you say you are in audit or share a report.

Before you flip the gate open, park a minimal answer bank: 30–50 canonical rows (access, encryption, IR, backups, subprocessors, support access) each with an evidence pointer. That way the first live questionnaire does not steal a week from GTM. Bridge letter + report help; reusable cited answers close the loop.

Trustfill is built for that async questionnaire draft-from-vault pattern: https://gettrustfill.com — soft mention; a spreadsheet with citations works as a starter too.

title: checklist: Enterprise sales readiness gate

## Team Building Timeline (Three Scenarios)

Our hiring plan scales with our ambition and funding. All scenarios share two immediate, non-negotiable hires.

### Non-Negotiable (All Scenarios — Needed NOW)

- [ ] **Mobile Native Engineer (Swift) — Contract** (March 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Build the iOS camera module that captures photo/video proof. This is a physical device feature that AI cannot build — it requires Xcode, a real iPhone, and Swift expertise.
  - **Why urgent:** The camera module is the #1 Beta blocker. Without it, users can't submit proof, and without proof, there's no product. Issues #123, #134.
  - **How to find:** iOS freelancer platforms (Toptal, Gun.io), Swift developer communities, contract through an agency. 2-3 month engagement.

- [ ] **Legal Counsel — Retainer** (March 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Review our custody model (how we hold user money), confirm which states we can operate in, review App Store submission materials, and eventually sign off on the skill-contest whitepaper.
  - **Why urgent:** Multiple Beta-phase tasks require legal sign-off. Issues #132, #133, #136, #146.
  - **How to find:** Fintech/gaming lawyers. Recommendations from founder networks, legal marketplaces.

### Conservative Scenario (Founders + 1 Contractor)
Minimal hiring. Founders handle support, growth, and business development themselves.

- [ ] **Kotlin contractor — short engagement** (May 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Build the Android version of the health data bridge (Google Health Connect). Short engagement, 1-2 months.
  - **Why:** Android parity for Gamma phase. iOS-only is fine for beta but not for public launch.

### Moderate Scenario (Founders + 2-3 Hires)

- [ ] **Mobile Native (Kotlin) — contract or part-time** (May 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Full Android parity — camera module, health bridge, push notifications.
- [ ] **QA / Beta Coordinator — part-time** (May 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Manage the TestFlight beta cohort, triage user feedback, organize bug reports, coordinate testing sessions. This person is the bridge between testers and engineers.
- [ ] **Growth / Community Manager** (September 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Build and manage our online community (Reddit, Discord), create content, manage partnerships with therapists/coaches. This is the person who turns users into advocates.

### Aggressive Scenario (Founders + 4-6 Hires, Assumes Fundraise)

- [ ] **Mobile Native (Kotlin) — full-time** (May 2026)
- [ ] **QA / Beta Coordinator — full-time** (May 2026)
- [ ] **Growth / Community Manager** (July 2026) — Earlier start for community building
- [ ] **Enterprise Sales / BD** (September 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Actively reach out to therapists and coaches, run pilots, negotiate contracts. This role drives B2B revenue.
- [ ] **DevOps / SRE — part-time** (October 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Production reliability, monitoring, auto-scaling, cost optimization. This role keeps the system running as we grow.
- [ ] **Content / Marketing** (October 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** SEO content, social media, PR for App Store launch. Full-time content creation.

comments.body:
Item (3) — pre-filled security questionnaire template — is where most teams get burned: they write beautiful prose once, then it drifts from the architecture/SLA docs in (1) and (2).

Practical approach: generate the questionnaire pack *from the same evidence vault* as the security posture doc. Each answer = short claim + citation (policy section, architecture note, SLA clause) + last-verified date. When encryption or uptime language changes, you refresh the cited source and the questionnaire rows update with it — no orphan Word file.

I work on Trustfill for that evidence→cited questionnaire draft loop: https://gettrustfill.com. Even manually, one vault with citations beats three diverging PDFs.

**Phase:** Beta (March 2026)

Recovery protocol guardrails are safety limits that prevent users in active recovery (like No-Contact after a breakup) from making impulsive decisions that hurt their progress.

This includes:
- Cooldown periods before changing contract terms
- Warnings when modifying commitments during emotionally vulnerable windows
- Rate limits on contract modifications

Think of it as the "are you sure?" system — but informed by behavioral science about when people are most likely to self-sabotage.

Partially implemented; needs completion and test coverage.

body:
**Phase:** Delta (August 2026)

RAIN stands for Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Non-identify — a mindfulness technique for handling difficult emotions.

When our system detects patterns suggesting a user is struggling (missed proofs, late-night app usage, rapid contract changes), it sends a gentle notification using the RAIN framework instead of a generic reminder. This turns Styx from a punitive accountability tool into a supportive one.

**Example:** instead of "You missed your proof!" → "We noticed you might be having a tough day. Here's a moment to pause: Recognize what you're feeling..."
title: feat: RAIN mindfulness notifications (F-AEGIS-08)

body:
**Phase:** Delta (September 2026)

The "Ostrich Effect" is a behavioral economics concept: people avoid checking their progress when they suspect bad news.

In Styx, this looks like a user who stops opening the app during difficult stretches — exactly when they need accountability most. This feature detects these avoidance patterns (declining login frequency, skipped proof windows, ignored notifications) and triggers supportive interventions before the user fully disengages.

Connected to churn signal detection in the Customer Success department tracker (#291).
title: feat: Ostrich effect detection (F-AEGIS-09)

body:
**Phase:** Gamma (June 2026)

Research shows people are more likely to complete a goal when the final stretch has higher stakes — the "finish line effect."

This feature lets users opt into doubling their stake for the last 20% of their contract. If they've committed $30 for a 30-day No-Contact challenge, they can choose to risk an additional $30 for the final 6 days.

The higher stakes during the most tempting period (when people think "I've already mostly done it, one text won't hurt") dramatically increases completion rates.

## Scope
- define the role split between Jessica and Styx
- lock CTA rules so both channels route into the same owned funnel
- implement the 30-day social cadence for both accounts
- keep Jessica discovery-led and Styx product-proof-led
- prevent the empty-room problem by routing audience into waitlist -> cohort -> ritual, not public community first

## Done When
- one approved channel architecture exists
- a 30-day calendar is ready for execution
- CTA ownership and audience routing are explicit
- the content split preserves the Phase 1 wedge and trust language

## Summary
Design the first lightweight referral loop for qualified beta growth without turning the launch into a public open-door system.

## Scope
- define the first referral mechanic for the waitlist or accepted cohort
- keep the mechanic simple: nominate 1-2 aligned friends or equivalent controlled invite flow
- decide what users see, what is tracked, and what qualifies as a valid referral
- ensure the referral motion supports small curated cohorts rather than vanity growth

## Done When
- the referral mechanic is defined in plain English
- referral eligibility and limits are explicit
- the required tracking and messaging are identified
- the output is ready for product/ops implementation follow-up
